key_insight: A stepped semi-coherent Cr-rich BCC/Ni-rich FCC eutectic interface with Ni segregation enables strain-gradient-driven dislocation activity, twinning, and interfacial shear, producing strong but ductile plastic co-deformation.
engineering_relevance: Designing ultrafine eutectic microstructures with controlled interlamellar spacing and interface chemistry could improve strength-ductility balance in additively manufactured structural alloys.
method: Atomic-resolution STEM/HR-STEM to map interface structure, orientation relationship, and local chemistry
In situ micro-compression and micro-tension testing in SEM to observe deformation and failure modes
Atomistic modeling to probe how interface structure/chemistry affects slip activation
evidence_signal: The abstract reports large plastic strains at high flow stress without cracking, with correlative microscopy showing a sequence of dislocation accumulation, twinning, interfacial shear, and later BCC dislocation activity.
watch_item: The mechanism is demonstrated on a specific additively manufactured Cr-Ni eutectic with ~450 nm spacing, so performance may depend strongly on processing window and interface stability.
paper_title: Strengthening and interface-mediated plastic co-deformation in an ultrafine Cr-Ni eutectic: A nanomechanical investigation
